title: Time Series Analysis Walkthrough: Python vs R
text:
Step 4: Statistical Tests for Stationarity
Before we fit any AR/MA-style model, let’s check whether the series is (close to) stationary — roughly constant mean and variance over time. Three checks:

Augmented Dickey-Fuller (ADF): null hypothesis is “non-stationary.” A small p-value lets us reject that, i.e. it argues for stationarity.
KPSS: the opposite null — “stationary.” A small p-value here argues against stationarity. Running both is a useful cross-check, since they can occasionally disagree.
Ljung-Box: tests whether there’s significant leftover autocorrelation at a given lag — useful here, and again later on model residuals.


PythonR



adf_raw = adfuller(train)
print(f"ADF on raw series  -> statistic={adf_raw[0]:.3f}, p-value={adf_raw[1]:.3f}")

ADF on raw series  -> statistic=0.888, p-value=0.993

kpss_raw = kpss(train, regression="c")
print(f"KPSS on raw series -> statistic={kpss_raw[0]:.3f}, p-value={kpss_raw[1]:.3f}")

KPSS on raw series -> statistic=1.886, p-value=0.010

lb_raw = acorr_ljungbox(train, lags=[12], return_df=True)
print("\nLjung-Box test (lag 12) on raw series:")


Ljung-Box test (lag 12) on raw series:

print(lb_raw)

       lb_stat      lb_pvalue
12  916.316804  1.798815e-188

# Stabilize the variance with a log transform, then remove the trend with one difference
log_train = np.log(train)
log_diff = log_train.diff().dropna()

adf_diff = adfuller(log_diff)
print(f"\nADF on log + differenced series -> statistic={adf_diff[0]:.3f}, p-value={adf_diff[1]:.3f}")


ADF on log + differenced series -> statistic=-3.065, p-value=0.029





adf_raw <- adf.test(train)
print(adf_raw)


    Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test

data:  train
Dickey-Fuller = -7.1692, Lag order = 5, p-value = 0.01
alternative hypothesis: stationary

kpss_raw <- kpss.test(train)
print(kpss_raw)


    KPSS Test for Level Stationarity

data:  train
KPSS Level = 2.5186, Truncation lag parameter = 4, p-value = 0.01

lb_raw <- Box.test(train, lag = 12, type = "Ljung-Box")
print(lb_raw)


    Box-Ljung test

data:  train
X-squared = 916.32, df = 12, p-value < 2.2e-16

# Stabilize the variance with a log transform, then remove the trend with one difference
log_train <- log(train)
log_diff <- diff(log_train)

adf_diff <- adf.test(log_diff)
print(adf_diff)


    Augmented Dickey-Fuller Test

data:  log_diff
Dickey-Fuller = -6.0845, Lag order = 5, p-value = 0.01
alternative hypothesis: stationary





On the raw training series, we’d expect the ADF p-value to come back large (can’t reject “non-stationary”) and the KPSS p-value to come back small (rejects “stationary”) — both pointing the same direction, matching the obvious trend in the plot. The Ljung-Box test should also come back with a tiny p-value, confirming there’s a lot of structure (trend + seasonality) still left to account for.
The log transform we just applied addresses the growing seasonal swings from Step 3 — working in log space turns a multiplicative pattern into an additive one, which is exactly the kind of pattern ARIMA-family models assume. Taking one difference on top of that removes the trend. After both steps, the ADF p-value on log_diff should drop well below 0.05 — that series is stationary enough to model directly. This is exactly what ARIMA(p, 1, q) is doing internally once we hand it the logged series with d = 1 in Step 7.

title: Time Series Analysis Walkthrough: Python vs R
text:
Step 5: Forecast With Classical Decomposition
Let’s see how far a simple, easy-to-explain forecast gets us, before we bring in ARIMA at all:

Fit a straight line through the trend component (from the training decomposition only) and extend it 12 months forward.
Repeat the last full seasonal cycle for those same 12 months.
Add the two together.
Check the result against the actual held-out 1960 values, using three error measures: RMSE, MAE, and MAPE (mean absolute percentage error).


PythonR



trend_comp = add_decomp.trend.dropna()
t_trend = np.arange(len(trend_comp))
slope, intercept = np.polyfit(t_trend, trend_comp.values, 1)

t_future = np.arange(len(trend_comp), len(trend_comp) + h)
trend_forecast = slope * t_future + intercept

seasonal_cycle = add_decomp.seasonal.values[-12:]
seasonal_forecast = np.tile(seasonal_cycle, h // 12 + 1)[:h]

decomp_forecast = trend_forecast + seasonal_forecast

rmse_decomp = np.sqrt(np.mean((decomp_forecast - test.values) ** 2))
mae_decomp = np.mean(np.abs(decomp_forecast - test.values))
mape_decomp = np.mean(np.abs((test.values - decomp_forecast) / test.values)) * 100

print("Classical decomposition forecast vs. actual 1960:")

Classical decomposition forecast vs. actual 1960:

print(f"  RMSE = {rmse_decomp:.1f} (thousands)")

  RMSE = 61.7 (thousands)

print(f"  MAE  = {mae_decomp:.1f} (thousands)")

  MAE  = 46.5 (thousands)

print(f"  MAPE = {mape_decomp:.1f}%")

  MAPE = 8.8%

plt.figure(figsize=(9, 4))
plt.plot(train.index, train.values, label="Train")
plt.plot(test.index, test.values, label="Actual 1960", color="black", linestyle="--")
plt.plot(test.index, decomp_forecast, label="Decomposition Forecast", color="darkorange")
plt.title("Classical Decomposition Forecast vs. Actual")
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()











trend_comp <- na.omit(decomp_add$trend)
t_trend <- seq_along(trend_comp)
fit <- lm(trend_comp ~ t_trend)

t_future <- seq(length(trend_comp) + 1, length(trend_comp) + h)
trend_forecast <- predict(fit, newdata = data.frame(t_trend = t_future))

# decompose() stores one repeating seasonal cycle in $figure
seasonal_forecast <- rep(decomp_add$figure, length.out = h)

decomp_forecast <- trend_forecast + seasonal_forecast

rmse_decomp <- sqrt(mean((decomp_forecast - as.numeric(test))^2))
mae_decomp <- mean(abs(decomp_forecast - as.numeric(test)))
mape_decomp <- mean(abs((as.numeric(test) - decomp_forecast) / as.numeric(test))) * 100

cat("Classical decomposition forecast vs. actual 1960:\n")

Classical decomposition forecast vs. actual 1960:

cat("  RMSE =", round(rmse_decomp, 1), "(thousands)\n")

  RMSE = 61.7 (thousands)

cat("  MAE  =", round(mae_decomp, 1), "(thousands)\n")

  MAE  = 46.5 (thousands)

cat("  MAPE =", round(mape_decomp, 1), "%\n")

  MAPE = 8.8 %

forecast_ts <- ts(decomp_forecast, start = c(1960, 1), frequency = 12)

ts.plot(train, test, forecast_ts,
        col = c("steelblue", "black", "darkorange"), lty = c(1, 2, 1),
        main = "Classical Decomposition Forecast vs. Actual")
legend("topleft", legend = c("Train", "Actual 1960", "Decomposition Forecast"),
       col = c("steelblue", "black", "darkorange"), lty = c(1, 2, 1))











The RMSE and MAE are in the same units as the data (thousands of monthly passengers), so they’re directly readable — an MAE around 45-50 means the forecast was off by roughly 45-50 thousand passengers per month on average over 1960. MAPE gives us the same idea as a percentage, which is handy for comparing across datasets later. Worth noticing on the chart too: because this forecast used the additive decomposition’s trend and seasonal figure, it tends to undershoot the biggest summer peaks a little — the same weakness we flagged for additive decomposition back in Step 3. Let’s keep that number in mind; we’ll compare it directly to ARIMA’s error in Step 9.

title: Time Series Analysis Walkthrough: Python vs R
section: Step 6: ACF and PACF — Choosing Model Orders
text:
Step 6: ACF and PACF — Choosing Model Orders
Now let’s switch over to ARMA-family models. We already stationarized the series in Step 4 (log_diff = one difference of the logged training data), so we can go straight to the ACF and PACF on that. These plots are normally how you’d pick starting values for p and q: bars that stick out past the shaded/dashed confidence band are the lags worth paying attention to.

PythonR



fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(10, 4))
plot_acf(log_diff, lags=24, ax=axes[0])
plot_pacf(log_diff, lags=24, ax=axes[1])
axes[0].set_title("ACF (log, differenced)")
axes[1].set_title("PACF (log, differenced)")
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()











par(mfrow = c(1, 2))
acf(log_diff, lag.max = 24, main = "ACF (log, differenced)")
pacf(log_diff, lag.max = 24, main = "PACF (log, differenced)")







par(mfrow = c(1, 1))




The early lags (1-3) usually show up as significant in both plots, which is typical of an ARMA-type process — that’s why an order like (2, ., 2) is a reasonable starting point in the next step. We’ll also likely spot a spike around lag 12 (maybe 24) — that’s the seasonal cycle poking through. A single ordinary difference removes the trend but not seasonality; that’s a real limitation of plain ARIMA on a series like this one, and it’s worth remembering once we get to the forecast comparison in Step 9.

title: Time Series Analysis Walkthrough: Python vs R
section: Step 7: Fit AR, MA, ARMA, and ARIMA Models
text:
Step 7: Fit AR, MA, ARMA, and ARIMA Models
Let’s fit all four model types — they’re really all special cases of ARIMA(p, d, q) — on the logged training data, for the variance- stabilizing reason discussed in Step 4:



Model
(p, d, q)




AR(2)
(2, 0, 0)


MA(2)
(0, 0, 2)


ARMA(2,2)
(2, 0, 2)


ARIMA(2,1,2)
(2, 1, 2)



We’ll compare them two ways: AIC (lower is better — rewards a closer fit but penalizes unnecessary extra parameters) and in-sample RMSE on the log scale (how far the model’s own fitted values were from the training data it was fit on).

PythonR



orders = {
    "AR(2)": (2, 0, 0),
    "MA(2)": (0, 0, 2),
    "ARMA(2,2)": (2, 0, 2),
    "ARIMA(2,1,2)": (2, 1, 2),
}

rows = []
for name, order in orders.items():
    p, d, q = order
    fitted_model = ARIMA(log_train, order=order).fit()
    # skip the first d points -- differencing leaves them without a real fitted value
    resid = log_train.iloc[d:] - fitted_model.fittedvalues.iloc[d:]
    rmse_log = np.sqrt(np.mean(resid ** 2))
    rows.append({"model": name, "AIC": fitted_model.aic, "in_sample_RMSE_log": rmse_log})

model_comparison = pd.DataFrame(rows).set_index("model")
print(model_comparison.round(4))

                   AIC  in_sample_RMSE_log
model                                     
AR(2)        -212.7221              0.1211
MA(2)         -89.1060              0.1773
ARMA(2,2)    -216.5726              0.1183
ARIMA(2,1,2) -226.2706              0.0981





orders <- list(
  "AR(2)"        = c(2, 0, 0),
  "MA(2)"        = c(0, 0, 2),
  "ARMA(2,2)"    = c(2, 0, 2),
  "ARIMA(2,1,2)" = c(2, 1, 2)
)

model_comparison <- t(sapply(orders, function(o) {
  fit <- arima(log_train, order = o)
  c(AIC = AIC(fit), in_sample_RMSE_log = sqrt(mean(fit$residuals^2)))
}))
print(round(model_comparison, 4))

                   AIC in_sample_RMSE_log
AR(2)        -212.7221             0.1037
MA(2)         -89.1060             0.1619
ARMA(2,2)    -216.5726             0.1005
ARIMA(2,1,2) -226.2706             0.0976





ARIMA(2,1,2) should come out clearly ahead of the other three on both AIC and in-sample RMSE, and that’s not a coincidence — AR(2) and MA(2) on their own have no mechanism for handling a strong, sustained trend like this one; only the d = 1 differencing term lets the model follow a moving level. That’s the practical reason ARIMA tends to beat plain AR or MA models on trending data, beyond just “it has more parameters.” (One small note: Python and R estimate these models slightly differently under the hood — conditional sum-of-squares vs. a state-space/Kalman filter approach by default — so don’t expect the two tables to match to the decimal. They should agree on which model wins, though.)

title: Time Series Analysis Walkthrough: Python vs R
text:
Step 8: Forecast With ARIMA
Let’s take ARIMA(2,1,2) — fit on the log scale — and forecast the same 12 held-out months. We’ll exponentiate the forecast (and its confidence interval) back to the original passenger-count scale before comparing it to test, using the same three error measures as Step 5.

PythonR



final_model = ARIMA(log_train, order=(2, 1, 2)).fit()
fc = final_model.get_forecast(steps=h)

fc_mean = np.exp(fc.predicted_mean)
ci = np.exp(fc.conf_int())

rmse_arima = np.sqrt(np.mean((fc_mean.values - test.values) ** 2))
mae_arima = np.mean(np.abs(fc_mean.values - test.values))
mape_arima = np.mean(np.abs((test.values - fc_mean.values) / test.values)) * 100

print("ARIMA(2,1,2) forecast vs. actual 1960:")

ARIMA(2,1,2) forecast vs. actual 1960:

print(f"  RMSE = {rmse_arima:.1f} (thousands)")

  RMSE = 93.8 (thousands)

print(f"  MAE  = {mae_arima:.1f} (thousands)")

  MAE  = 67.9 (thousands)

print(f"  MAPE = {mape_arima:.1f}%")

  MAPE = 12.7%

plt.figure(figsize=(9, 4))
plt.plot(train.index, train.values, label="Train")
plt.plot(test.index, test.values, label="Actual 1960", color="black", linestyle="--")
plt.plot(test.index, fc_mean.values, label="ARIMA Forecast", color="darkgreen")
plt.fill_between(test.index, ci.iloc[:, 0], ci.iloc[:, 1], color="darkgreen", alpha=0.2, label="95% CI")
plt.title("ARIMA(2,1,2) Forecast (back-transformed from log scale) vs. Actual")
plt.legend()
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()











final_fit <- arima(log_train, order = c(2, 1, 2))
pred <- predict(final_fit, n.ahead = h)

upper_log <- pred$pred + 1.96 * pred$se
lower_log <- pred$pred - 1.96 * pred$se

fc_mean <- exp(pred$pred)
upper <- exp(upper_log)
lower <- exp(lower_log)

rmse_arima <- sqrt(mean((as.numeric(fc_mean) - as.numeric(test))^2))
mae_arima <- mean(abs(as.numeric(fc_mean) - as.numeric(test)))
mape_arima <- mean(abs((as.numeric(test) - as.numeric(fc_mean)) / as.numeric(test))) * 100

cat("ARIMA(2,1,2) forecast vs. actual 1960:\n")

ARIMA(2,1,2) forecast vs. actual 1960:

cat("  RMSE =", round(rmse_arima, 1), "(thousands)\n")

  RMSE = 93.8 (thousands)

cat("  MAE  =", round(mae_arima, 1), "(thousands)\n")

  MAE  = 67.9 (thousands)

cat("  MAPE =", round(mape_arima, 1), "%\n")

  MAPE = 12.7 %

forecast_ts <- ts(as.numeric(fc_mean), start = c(1960, 1), frequency = 12)
upper_ts <- ts(as.numeric(upper), start = c(1960, 1), frequency = 12)
lower_ts <- ts(as.numeric(lower), start = c(1960, 1), frequency = 12)

ts.plot(train, test, forecast_ts, upper_ts, lower_ts,
        col = c("steelblue", "black", "darkgreen", "darkgreen", "darkgreen"),
        lty = c(1, 2, 1, 2, 2),
        main = "ARIMA(2,1,2) Forecast (back-transformed from log scale) vs. Actual")
legend("topleft", legend = c("Train", "Actual 1960", "ARIMA Forecast", "95% CI"),
       col = c("steelblue", "black", "darkgreen", "darkgreen"), lty = c(1, 2, 1, 2))











Hang on to these three numbers — we’re comparing them directly to Step 5’s in the next step.

title: Time Series Analysis Walkthrough: Python vs R
text:
Step 9: Compare Forecast Accuracy
Let’s put the two forecasts side by side using the error numbers we collected in Steps 5 and 8.

PythonR



accuracy_comparison = pd.DataFrame(
    {
        "RMSE": [rmse_decomp, rmse_arima],
        "MAE": [mae_decomp, mae_arima],
        "MAPE (%)": [mape_decomp, mape_arima],
    },
    index=["Classical Decomposition", "ARIMA(2,1,2)"],
)
print(accuracy_comparison.round(2))

                          RMSE   MAE  MAPE (%)
Classical Decomposition  61.70  46.5      8.77
ARIMA(2,1,2)             93.85  67.9     12.73





accuracy_comparison <- data.frame(
  RMSE = c(rmse_decomp, rmse_arima),
  MAE = c(mae_decomp, mae_arima),
  MAPE = c(mape_decomp, mape_arima)
)
rownames(accuracy_comparison) <- c("Classical Decomposition", "ARIMA(2,1,2)")
print(round(accuracy_comparison, 2))

                         RMSE  MAE  MAPE
Classical Decomposition 61.70 46.5  8.77
ARIMA(2,1,2)            93.85 67.9 12.73





Don’t be surprised if ARIMA(2,1,2) doesn’t clearly beat the much simpler decomposition forecast here — and if anything, it can come out worse. That’s not a bug; it’s the seasonal gap we flagged back in Step 6. Plain ARIMA(p, d, q) has no seasonal term, so on a series with a strong, regular 12-month cycle like this one, it’s working at a real disadvantage compared to a method (like our decomposition forecast) that explicitly repeats the seasonal pattern. The natural next step — adding a seasonal term to get a full SARIMA model — would very likely close that gap, but it’s outside the AR/MA/ARMA/ARIMA scope of this walkthrough. Worth having ready if someone in the room asks “why didn’t ARIMA win?”

title: Time Series Analysis Walkthrough: Python vs R
text:
Wrap-Up
We worked through the same nine steps twice — load, plot, decompose, test for stationarity, forecast with decomposition, check ACF/PACF, fit AR/MA/ARMA/ ARIMA, forecast with ARIMA, and compare. A few things that stood out as we went:

R’s ts object carries its frequency and start date with it everywhere; in pandas we manage a DatetimeIndex ourselves, which is one extra step but also more flexible for irregular data.
R’s decompose() and arima() are base functions — nothing to install beyond tseries for the stationarity tests. Python relies on statsmodels for essentially all of it.
decompose() hands us the seasonal index directly as $figure; in statsmodels we get there by slicing the first 12 values out of the repeating seasonal series — same number, different route.
predict() on an R arima fit returns log-scale predictions and standard errors we exponentiate by hand; statsmodels’ get_forecast().conf_int() gives a ready-made interval, still on whatever scale we fit on.
Plotting is base R graphics (plot(), acf(), ts.plot()) versus matplotlib — different calls, but once we know the data lines up, it’s a fairly direct line-by-line translation between the two.

1.1 History and Origin

1.1.1 Guido van Rossum and the Birth of Python



Guido van Rossum — Creator of Python (photo: Dan Stroud, CC BY)





👨‍💻

Created by
Guido van Rossum



📅

First release
February 1991



🏛️

Origin
CWI, Netherlands



🎭

Named after
Monty Python comedy


Python was created by Guido van Rossum, a Dutch programmer, who began work on it in December 1989 at Centrum Wiskunde & Informatica (CWI) in the Netherlands. The first public release — Python 0.9.0 — appeared in February 1991.






Note🎭 Why the name “Python”?



The name has nothing to do with the snake. Van Rossum was a fan of Monty Python’s Flying Circus and wanted a short, unique, slightly quirky name. The snake branding came much later — and stuck!



Key milestones in Python’s history:












1.1.2 Python 2 vs Python 3






Important⛔ Always Use Python 3 — No Exceptions



Python 2 reached End-of-Life on 1 January 2020. All major scientific libraries (NumPy, pandas, SciPy, scikit-learn) have dropped Python 2 support. Install Python 3 — there is absolutely no reason to install Python 2 for new work.

✅ Actively developed






1.1.3 Interpreted Language — What Does That Mean for Statistics?
Python is an interpreted language — code runs line-by-line, just like R. This is ideal for statistical work:



⚡

Interactive

🔄

🧪

🤝

Code
# Run this line-by-line in any Python interpreter or Jupyter cell
import math
data       = [12.4, 15.6, 10.8, 14.2]
mean_val   = sum(data) / len(data)
variance   = sum((x - mean_val)**2 for x in data) / len(data)
std_approx = math.sqrt(variance)
print(f"Mean: {mean_val:.2f}   SD: {std_approx:.2f}")
# → Mean: 13.25   SD: 1.81

1.2 Key Features of Python

1.2.1 📖 Readable and Simple Syntax
Python enforces indentation (4 spaces) instead of braces {}. Code reads like structured English — critical when statisticians who are not software engineers need to read and verify the code.


Code
# ── Compute a one-sample t-statistic from scratch ─────────────────
# Anyone can follow this, even without a Python background
sample   = [22.1, 19.8, 23.4, 21.0, 20.5]
n        = len(sample)
mean     = sum(sample) / n
variance = sum((x - mean)**2 for x in sample) / (n - 1)   # Bessel's correction
std_err  = (variance / n) ** 0.5

t_stat   = (mean - 20) / std_err    # H₀: population mean = 20
print(f"n={n}   mean={mean:.2f}   t={t_stat:.3f}")

text:
2.3 pip and conda — Package Managers

2.3.1 What is a Package Manager?
A package manager is like an app store for Python libraries — it installs, updates, and removes packages and their dependencies automatically.



🐍

pip
Official Python manager, installs from PyPI



🅲

conda
Anaconda’s manager, handles compiled libraries



🏪

PyPI
500,000+ packages at pypi.org



🌿

conda-forge
Community-maintained conda packages





2.3.2 conda — The Anaconda Way
conda manages both packages and environments, and handles non-Python compiled dependencies (C/Fortran libraries) that pip can’t always handle.


Code
conda search scipy                           # Find a package
conda install scipy                          # Install into current environment
conda install scipy=1.11.0                   # Install specific version
conda install numpy pandas matplotlib seaborn  # Install multiple packages
conda update scipy                           # Update one package
conda update --all                           # Update everything
conda remove scipy                           # Remove a package
conda list                                   # Show all installed packages
conda env list                               # Show all environments




2.3.3 pip — The Python Standard Manager


Code
pip install statsmodels                      # Install from PyPI
pip install statsmodels==0.14.0             # Specific version
pip install -r requirements.txt             # Install from a requirements file
pip install --upgrade statsmodels           # Upgrade
pip uninstall statsmodels                   # Remove
pip list                                    # List all installed
pip show numpy                              # Details about one package




2.3.4 conda vs pip — Decision Table






Warning⚠️ Best Practice: conda first, pip as fallback



Inside a conda environment, prefer conda install. Only use pip for packages not available via conda, and do so after all conda installs. Mixing carelessly can break environments.





Situation
Use




Scientific packages (NumPy, SciPy, pandas)
✅ conda install


Package only on PyPI, not in conda
✅ pip install


Managing isolated project environments
✅ conda create


Compiled C/Fortran extensions
✅ conda preferred


Niche or personal packages
✅ pip install






2.3.5 Virtual Environments — Why They Matter
A virtual environment is an isolated Python installation. Each project gets its own Python version and package versions — they never interfere with each other.











Code
# Create a fresh environment for your statistics project
conda create --name stats_project python=3.11

# Activate it (your prompt changes to: (stats_project) $)
conda activate stats_project

# Install all packages you need FOR THIS PROJECT ONLY
conda install numpy pandas scipy statsmodels matplotlib seaborn jupyter

# When done working, deactivate
conda deactivate

# Delete when no longer needed
conda env remove --name stats_project

3.1.3.3 Magic Commands — Special Powers

%config InlineBackend.figure_format = 'retina'   # for crisp charts on high-resolution screens

Important🔁 Always “Restart & Run All” before submitting or sharing



The kernel is the Python process behind your notebook. Running cells out of order is a common source of errors. Before sharing your notebook, go to:

section: 3.4 Putting It All Together — Your First Statistical Session
text:
3.4 Putting It All Together — Your First Statistical Session






Note🚀 Complete Workflow: Launch → Analyse → Visualise



Follow these steps to run your first real statistical analysis in Python.



3.4.1 Step 1 — Launch your environment


Code
conda activate stats_project    # Activate your environment
cd ~/Documents/stats_project    # Navigate to your project
jupyter lab                     # Open JupyterLab




3.4.2 Step 2 — Set up your notebook


Code
# Cell 1: Import all libraries
import numpy as np
import pandas as pd
import scipy.stats as stats
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import seaborn as sns

sns.set_theme(style="whitegrid", palette="deep")
%matplotlib inline
%config InlineBackend.figure_format = 'retina'

print("✅ Libraries loaded successfully!")
print(f"   numpy   {np.__version__}")
print(f"   pandas  {pd.__version__}")




3.4.3 Step 3 — Create data and run a t-test


Code
# Cell 2: Simulate two groups and run an independent t-test
np.random.seed(2024)
control   = np.random.normal(loc=50, scale=10, size=30)
treatment = np.random.normal(loc=56, scale=10, size=30)

t_stat, p_value = stats.ttest_ind(control, treatment)
print(f"t-statistic = {t_stat:.3f}")
print(f"p-value     = {p_value:.4f}")
print("→ Statistically significant!" if p_value < 0.05 else "→ Not significant at α = 0.05")




3.4.4 Step 4 — Visualise


Code
# Cell 3: Boxplot + strip plot
fig, axes = plt.subplots(1, 2, figsize=(11, 5))

# Left: Boxplot
axes[0].boxplot([control, treatment], labels=["Control", "Treatment"],
                patch_artist=True,
                boxprops=dict(facecolor="#dbeafe", color="#1d4ed8"),
                medianprops=dict(color="#dc2626", linewidth=2))
axes[0].set_ylabel("Score")
axes[0].set_title(f"Independent t-test\nt = {t_stat:.2f},  p = {p_value:.4f}")

# Right: Distributions
x = np.linspace(20, 90, 300)
for grp, col, lbl in [(control,"#3b82f6","Control"),(treatment,"#ef4444","Treatment")]:
    mu, sd = grp.mean(), grp.std(ddof=1)
    axes[1].plot(x, stats.norm.pdf(x, mu, sd), linewidth=2.5, label=f"{lbl} (μ={mu:.1f})", color=col)
    axes[1].hist(grp, bins=12, density=True, alpha=0.25, color=col)
axes[1].set_xlabel("Score")
axes[1].set_ylabel("Density")
axes[1].set_title("Overlapping Distributions")
axes[1].legend()

plt.suptitle("First Statistical Analysis in Python", fontsize=13, fontweight="bold", y=1.02)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()

title: Data Manipulation — Python (Pandas) vs R
text:
Pandas is an open-source Python library built on top of NumPy for fast, flexible data manipulation and analysis. Created by Wes McKinney in 2008, it is the de facto standard for tabular data work in Python.
Its two core data structures are:

Series — a 1-D labeled array (like a spreadsheet column)
DataFrame — a 2-D labeled table (like an Excel sheet or SQL table)












Use Case
Pandas
R Equivalent




Data loading
pd.read_csv(), pd.read_excel()
read.csv(), readr::read_csv()


Data cleaning
.dropna(), .fillna()
na.omit(), tidyr::fill()


Transformation
.merge(), .pivot()
dplyr::join*(), tidyr::pivot_*()


Exploration
.groupby(), .describe()
dplyr::group_by(), summary()


Time series

title: Data Manipulation — Python (Pandas) vs R
text:
Pandas is an open-source Python library built on top of NumPy for fast, flexible data manipulation and analysis. Created by Wes McKinney in 2008, it is the de facto standard for tabular data work in Python.
Its two core data structures are:

Series — a 1-D labeled array (like a spreadsheet column)
DataFrame — a 2-D labeled table (like an Excel sheet or SQL table)












Use Case
Pandas
R Equivalent

pd.read_csv(), pd.read_excel()
read.csv(), readr::read_csv()


Data cleaning
.dropna(), .fillna()
na.omit(), tidyr::fill()


Transformation
.merge(), .pivot()
dplyr::join*(), tidyr::pivot_*()


Exploration
.groupby(), .describe()
dplyr::group_by(), summary()


Time series
pd.DatetimeIndex

Python

import pandas as pd

pd.__version__
#> '3.0.2'

title: Data Manipulation — Python (Pandas) vs R
text:
3 The Series Object
A Pandas Series is a 1-D labeled array. The R equivalent is a named vector.

3.1 Creating

PythonR


# From a list (auto integer index)
s = pd.Series([0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0])
print(s)
#> 0    0.25
#> 1    0.50
#> 2    0.75
#> 3    1.00
#> dtype: float64
# From a dict (named index)
population = pd.Series({
    'California': 38332521,
    'Texas':      26448193,
    'New York':   19651127
})
print(population)
#> California    38332521
#> Texas         26448193
#> New York      19651127
#> dtype: int64


# Unnamed vector
s <- c(0.25, 0.5, 0.75, 1.0)
s
#> [1] 0.25 0.50 0.75 1.00
# Named vector (≈ dict-based Series)
population <- c(California = 38332521,
                Texas      = 26448193,
                "New York" = 19651127)
population
#> California      Texas   New York
#>   38332521   26448193   19651127





3.2 Indexing & Slicing








Operation
Python
R




By label
population['California']
population["California"]


By position
population.iloc[0]
population[1] (1-indexed)


Slice (label)
population['California':'Texas'] (inclusive)
population[c("California","Texas")]


Slice (position)
s[0:2] (excludes end)
s[1:2] (includes end)


Boolean
s[s > 0.5]
s[s > 0.5]


Names / Index
population.index
names(population)




PythonR


print(population['California'])        # by label
print(population.iloc[0])             # by position
print(population['California':'Texas'])# label slice (both ends inclusive)
print(s[s > 0.5])                     # boolean mask
#> 38332521
#> 38332521
#> California    38332521
#> Texas         26448193
#> dtype: int64
#> 2    0.75
#> 3    1.00
#> dtype: float64


population["California"]              # by label
population[1]                         # by position (1-indexed)
population[c("California","Texas")]   # label slice
s[s > 0.5]                           # boolean mask
#> California
#>   38332521
#> California
#>   38332521
#> California      Texas
#>   38332521   26448193
#> [1] 0.75 1.00









Important



Indexing difference: Python is 0-based; R is 1-based. Python label slices are inclusive on both ends; positional slices exclude the end. R slices are always inclusive.

title: Data Manipulation — Python (Pandas) vs R
text:
4 The DataFrame Object
A DataFrame is a 2-D labeled table — rows and columns both have labels.

4.1 Creating

PythonR


area = pd.Series({'California': 423967, 'Texas': 695662,
                  'New York': 141297,   'Florida': 170312})
pop  = pd.Series({'California': 38332521, 'Texas': 26448193,
                  'New York': 19651127,   'Florida': 19552860})

states = pd.DataFrame({'population': pop, 'area': area})
print(states)
#>             population    area
#> California    38332521  423967
#> Texas         26448193  695662
#> New York      19651127  141297
#> Florida       19552860  170312


states <- data.frame(
  population = c(California=38332521, Texas=26448193,
                 "New York"=19651127, Florida=19552860),
  area       = c(California=423967,   Texas=695662,
                 "New York"=141297,   Florida=170312)
)
states
#>            population   area
#> California   38332521 423967
#> Texas        26448193 695662
#> New York     19651127 141297
#> Florida      19552860 170312





4.2 Essential Operations








Operation
Python
R




Column access
states['area']
states$area


Add column
states['density'] = states['population'] / states['area']
states$density <- states$population / states$area


Dimensions
states.shape → (4, 2)
dim(states) → 4 2


Column names
states.columns
colnames(states)


Row names
states.index
rownames(states)


Transpose
states.T
t(states)




PythonR


# Add a derived column
states['density'] = states['population'] / states['area']
print(states)
print("\nshape:", states.shape)
#>             population    area     density
#> California    38332521  423967   90.413926
#> Texas         26448193  695662   38.018740
#> New York      19651127  141297  139.076746
#> Florida       19552860  170312  114.806121
#>
#> shape: (4, 3)


# Add a derived column
states$density <- states$population / states$area
states
cat("dim:", dim(states), "\n")
#>            population   area   density
#> California   38332521 423967  90.41393
#> Texas        26448193 695662  38.01874
#> New York     19651127 141297 139.07675
#> Florida      19552860 170312 114.80612
#>
#> dim: 4 3





4.3 Constructing from Other Sources

PythonR


# From list of dicts — missing keys become NaN
pd.DataFrame([{'a': 1, 'b': 2}, {'b': 3, 'c': 4}])
#>      a  b    c
#> 0  1.0  2  NaN
#> 1  NaN  3  4.0
# From NumPy array
np.random.seed(42)
pd.DataFrame(np.random.rand(3, 2), columns=['foo', 'bar'])
#>         foo       bar
#> 0  0.374540  0.950714
#> 1  0.731994  0.598658
#> 2  0.156019  0.155995


# From list — missing values become NA
data.frame(a=c(1,NA), b=c(2,3), c=c(NA,4))
#>    a b  c
#> 1  1 2 NA
#> 2 NA 3  4
# From matrix
set.seed(42)
matrix(runif(6), nrow=3, ncol=2) |>
  as.data.frame() |> setNames(c("foo","bar"))
#>         foo       bar
#> 1 0.9148060 0.8304476
#> 2 0.9370754 0.6417455
#> 3 0.2861395 0.5190959

title: Data Manipulation — Python (Pandas) vs R
text:
5 Data Indexing & Selection

5.1 Three Ways to Index a DataFrame (Python)








Indexer
Selects by
Example




df['col']
Column label
states['area']


df.loc[row, col]
Label (both axes)
states.loc['Texas', 'area']


df.iloc[row, col]
Integer position (both axes)
states.iloc[1, 1]



R uses df[row, col] for both — strings for labels, integers for positions.


5.2 Position-based & Label-based

PythonR


# First 3 rows, first 2 columns — position-based
states.iloc[:3, :2]
#>             population    area
#> California    38332521  423967
#> Texas         26448193  695662
#> New York      19651127  141297
# Label-based: specific row + column
states.loc['Texas', 'area']
#> 695662


# First 3 rows, first 2 columns — position-based
states[1:3, 1:2]
#>            population   area
#> California   38332521 423967
#> Texas        26448193 695662
#> New York     19651127 141297
# Label-based: specific row + column
states["Texas", "area"]
#> [1] 695662





5.3 Boolean Masking & Column Selection

PythonR


# Rows where density > 100, select two columns
states.loc[states['density'] > 100, ['population', 'density']]
#>           population     density
#> New York    19651127  139.076746
#> Florida     19552860  114.806121


# Base R
states[states$density > 100, c("population", "density")]
#>          population  density
#> New York   19651127 139.0767
#> Florida    19552860 114.8061
# dplyr (more readable for complex filters)
states %>% filter(density > 100) %>% select(population, density)
#>          population  density
#> New York   19651127 139.0767
#> Florida    19552860 114.8061









Important



Key difference: Python’s loc (label) and iloc (position) are explicit separate indexers. R uses [row, col] for both — the type of value (string vs integer) determines the mode.

title: Data Manipulation — Python (Pandas) vs R
text:
6 Operating on Data

6.1 NumPy UFuncs Preserve Labels
Pandas operations preserve index and column labels automatically.

PythonR


ser = pd.Series([6, 3, 7, 4])
np.exp(ser)           # index preserved
#> 0     403.428793
#> 1      20.085537
#> 2    1096.633158
#> 3      54.598150
#> dtype: float64


ser <- c(6, 3, 7, 4)
round(exp(ser), 4)    # names preserved if named
#> [1]  403.4288   20.0855 1096.6332   54.5982





6.2 Automatic Index Alignment
This is one of Pandas’ most powerful features — and one R does not do automatically.

PythonR


area = pd.Series({'Alaska': 1723337, 'Texas': 695662, 'California': 423967})
pop  = pd.Series({'California': 38332521, 'Texas': 26448193, 'New York': 19651127})

pop / area    # unmatched keys fill with NaN — automatically
#> Alaska              NaN
#> California    90.413926
#> New York            NaN
#> Texas         38.018740
#> dtype: float64


area <- c(Alaska=1723337, Texas=695662, California=423967)
pop  <- c(California=38332521, Texas=26448193, "New York"=19651127)

# R does NOT auto-align — manual intersection required
common <- intersect(names(pop), names(area))
pop[common] / area[common]
#> California      Texas
#>   90.41393   38.01874









Tip



Pandas fill value trick: Use A.add(B, fill_value=0) to treat missing index entries as 0 rather than NaN when adding two Series.




6.3 Arithmetic Method Equivalents



Python operator
Pandas method
R




+
.add(fill_value=)
+


-
.sub()
-


*
.mul()
*


/
.div()
/


//
.floordiv()
%/%


**
.pow()
^

title: Data Manipulation — Python (Pandas) vs R
text:
7 Handling Missing Data

7.1 How Missing Values Are Represented




Python (Pandas)
R




General
np.nan / None
NA


Float
np.nan (IEEE 754)
NA_real_


Integer
⚠️ Upcasts column to float64
NA_integer_ (native)


String
None
NA_character_









Important



R has typed NAs — an integer column stays integer even with missing values. Pandas converts integer columns to float64 when any NaN is introduced.



PythonR


# Integer array becomes float64 when NaN added
vals = np.array([1, np.nan, 3, 4])
print("dtype:", vals.dtype)

s = pd.Series([1, np.nan, 2, None])
print(s)
#> dtype: float64
#> 0    1.0
#> 1    NaN
#> 2    2.0
#> 3    NaN
#> dtype: float64


# Integer stays integer with NA
vals <- c(1L, NA_integer_, 3L, 4L)
cat("class:", class(vals), "\n")

s <- c(1, NA, 2, NA)
print(s)
#> class: integer
#> [1]  1 NA  2 NA





7.2 Detecting, Dropping & Filling

PythonR


df = pd.DataFrame([[1, np.nan, 2],
                   [2, 3,      5],
                   [np.nan, 4, 6]])

# Detect
print(df.isnull())

# Drop rows with ANY missing
print(df.dropna())

# Drop cols with ANY missing
print(df.dropna(axis='columns'))

# Fill with constant
print(df.fillna(0))

# Forward / back fill
s = pd.Series([1, np.nan, 2, np.nan, 3], index=list('abcde'))
print(s.ffill())
print(s.bfill())
#>        0      1      2
#> 0  False   True  False
#> 1  False  False  False
#> 2   True  False  False
#>
#>      0    1  2
#> 1  2.0  3.0  5
#>
#>    2
#> 0  2
#> 1  5
#> 2  6
#>
#>      0    1  2
#> 0  1.0  0.0  2
#> 1  2.0  3.0  5
#> 2  0.0  4.0  6
#>
#> a    1.0
#> b    1.0
#> c    2.0
#> d    2.0
#> e    3.0
#> dtype: float64
#> a    1.0
#> b    2.0
#> c    2.0
#> d    3.0
#> e    3.0
#> dtype: float64


df <- data.frame(V1=c(1,2,NA), V2=c(NA,3,4), V3=c(2,5,6))

# Detect
print(is.na(df))

# Drop rows with ANY missing
print(na.omit(df))

# Drop cols with ANY missing
print(df[, colSums(is.na(df)) == 0, drop=FALSE])

# Fill with constant
df_fill <- df; df_fill[is.na(df_fill)] <- 0
print(df_fill)

# Forward / back fill (tidyr)
data_fill <- c(a=1, b=NA, c=2, d=NA, e=3)
print(fill(data.frame(x=data_fill), x, .direction="down")$x)
print(fill(data.frame(x=data_fill), x, .direction="up")$x)
#>       V1    V2    V3
#> [1,] FALSE  TRUE FALSE
#> [2,] FALSE FALSE FALSE
#> [3,]  TRUE FALSE FALSE
#>
#>   V1 V2 V3
#> 2  2  3  5
#>
#>   V3
#> 1  2
#> 2  5
#> 3  6
#>
#>   V1 V2 V3
#> 1  1  0  2
#> 2  2  3  5
#> 3  0  4  6
#>
#> [1] 1 1 2 2 3
#> [1] 1 2 2 3 3

title: Data Manipulation — Python (Pandas) vs R
text:
8 Hierarchical Indexing
Pandas MultiIndex stores multi-level labels on rows or columns. R uses a long-format data frame with grouping columns and tidyr for reshaping.

PythonR


# Create MultiIndex Series
index = pd.MultiIndex.from_product(
    [['California','New York','Texas'], [2000, 2010]],
    names=['state', 'year'])
pop = pd.Series(
    [33871648, 37253956, 18976457, 19378102, 20851820, 25145561],
    index=index)
print(pop)
#> state       year
#> California  2000    33871648
#>             2010    37253956
#> New York    2000    18976457
#>             2010    19378102
#> Texas       2000    20851820
#>             2010    25145561
#> dtype: int64
# Partial index & unstack → wide table
print(pop['California'])          # one state
print(pop.unstack())              # long → wide
#> year
#> 2000    33871648
#> 2010    37253956
#> dtype: int64
#>
#> year            2000      2010
#> state
#> California  33871648  37253956
#> New York    18976457  19378102
#> Texas       20851820  25145561


# Equivalent: long-format data frame
pop <- data.frame(
  state      = rep(c("California","New York","Texas"), each=2),
  year       = rep(c(2000, 2010), times=3),
  population = c(33871648,37253956,18976457,19378102,20851820,25145561))
print(pop)
#>        state year population
#> 1 California 2000   33871648
#> 2 California 2010   37253956
#> 3   New York 2000   18976457
#> 4   New York 2010   19378102
#> 5      Texas 2000   20851820
#> 6      Texas 2010   25145561
# Filter one group (≈ partial index)
pop %>% filter(state == "California")

# Long → wide (≈ unstack)
pop %>% pivot_wider(names_from=year, values_from=population)
#>        state year population
#> 1 California 2000   33871648
#> 2 California 2010   37253956
#>
#>        state     2000     2010
#> 1 California 33871648 37253956
#> 2   New York 18976457 19378102
#> 3      Texas 20851820 25145561









Tip



unstack() / stack() in Pandas ↔︎ pivot_wider() / pivot_longer() in tidyr — same concept, different syntax.

title: Data Manipulation — Python (Pandas) vs R
text:
9 Combining Datasets: Concat & Append

9.1 pd.concat() — the one function for all stacking

PythonR


def make_df(cols, ind):
    return pd.DataFrame(
        {c: [str(c)+str(i) for i in ind] for c in cols}, ind)

df1 = make_df('AB', [1,2]); df2 = make_df('AB', [3,4])
df3 = make_df('AB', [0,1]); df4 = make_df('CD', [0,1])

# Row-wise (default axis=0)
print(pd.concat([df1, df2]))
#>     A   B
#> 1  A1  B1
#> 2  A2  B2
#> 3  A3  B3
#> 4  A4  B4
# Column-wise (axis=1)
print(pd.concat([df3, df4], axis=1))
#>     A   B   C   D
#> 0  A0  B0  C0  D0
#> 1  A1  B1  C1  D1
# Mismatched columns — outer join fills NaN (default)
df5 = make_df('ABC',[1,2]); df6 = make_df('BCD',[3,4])
print(pd.concat([df5, df6]))          # outer (default)
print(pd.concat([df5, df6], join='inner'))  # inner: shared cols only
#>      A   B   C    D
#> 1   A1  B1  C1  NaN
#> 2   A2  B2  C2  NaN
#> 3  NaN  B3  C3   D3
#> 4  NaN  B4  C4   D4
#>
#>     B   C
#> 1  B1  C1
#> 2  B2  C2
#> 3  B3  C3
#> 4  B4  C4
# Label source rows with keys → creates MultiIndex
print(pd.concat([df1, df2], keys=['x','y']))
#>       A   B
#> x 1  A1  B1
#>   2  A2  B2
#> y 3  A3  B3
#>   4  A4  B4


df1 <- data.frame(A=c("A1","A2"), B=c("B1","B2"))
df2 <- data.frame(A=c("A3","A4"), B=c("B3","B4"))
df3 <- data.frame(A=c("A0","A1"), B=c("B0","B1"))
df4 <- data.frame(C=c("C0","C1"), D=c("D0","D1"))

# Row-bind (≈ axis=0 concat)
rbind(df1, df2)
#>    A  B
#> 1 A1 B1
#> 2 A2 B2
#> 3 A3 B3
#> 4 A4 B4
# Column-bind (≈ axis=1 concat)
cbind(df3, df4)
#>    A  B  C  D
#> 1 A0 B0 C0 D0
#> 2 A1 B1 C1 D1
# Mismatched columns — bind_rows fills NA; rbind() would error
df5 <- data.frame(A=c("A1","A2"), B=c("B1","B2"), C=c("C1","C2"))
df6 <- data.frame(B=c("B3","B4"), C=c("C3","C4"), D=c("D3","D4"))
dplyr::bind_rows(df5, df6)        # outer (fills NA)
#>      A  B  C    D
#> 1   A1 B1 C1 <NA>
#> 2   A2 B2 C2 <NA>
#> 3 <NA> B3 C3   D3
#> 4 <NA> B4 C4   D4
# Inner — keep shared columns only
shared <- intersect(names(df5), names(df6))
rbind(df5[, shared], df6[, shared])
#>    B  C
#> 1 B1 C1
#> 2 B2 C2
#> 3 B3 C3
#> 4 B4 C4
# Source labelling (≈ keys=)
df1$source <- "x"; df2$source <- "y"
dplyr::bind_rows(df1, df2)
#>    A  B source
#> 1 A1 B1      x
#> 2 A2 B2      x
#> 3 A3 B3      y
#> 4 A4 B4      y









Warning



rbind() vs bind_rows(): Base R rbind() errors on mismatched columns. dplyr::bind_rows() fills missing columns with NA, matching pd.concat() outer-join behaviour.








Note



DataFrame.append() is gone. It was deprecated in Pandas 1.4 and removed in Pandas 2.0. Always use pd.concat().
